figures.caption: Figure 1: Experimental results. Row 1: Synthetic regression (costs different). Row 2: MIMIC EHR-discharge consistency (costs different). Row 3: Hypothyroid detection (costs equal). Row 4: VeriFact-BHC proposition consistency (costs equal). Left panels show CI width as a function of total budget; middle panels show empirical coverage with 90% target (dashed); right panels show the AM-PPI / ASI CI-width ratio (%), where values below 100% indicate AM-PPI produces narrower intervals than the baseline. Each method’s curve begins at the smallest budget where it can fund its predictor queries plus a minimum gold-label sample (Appendix F). Shaded bands show ±1 standard error of the mean (SEM) across independent trials.

Figure 2: CI width reduction (%) under the two-population model. Left: Case 1 (AM-PPI versus ASI using the expensive predictor) depends only on the fraction of easy examples p and φ = c2/b. Right: Case 2 at p = 0.5 (AM-PPI versus ASI using the cheap predictor) under c1 ≪ c2 and re ≪ rh; AM-PPI improves upon ASI where δ > (1− p)φ.

Figure 3: Synthetic mechanism analysis. Each panel sweeps total budget B for one configuration of the cheap and expensive cost ratio, accuracy ratio, candidate subset family I, and routing-error rate; predictors and uncertainty surrogate are oracles so all observed effects are attributable to the AM-PPI optimization itself. Panels (a) through (e) build incrementally to the cost-plus-accuracy regime that produces the three-region pattern observed in Figure 1; panel (f) verifies that random routing collapses AM-PPI onto the ASI baselines. Shaded bands show ±1 SEM across 10 Bernoulli draws per budget.
